speaker: interviewer
text: It got slated in our case.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I know I didn't slate the last one. I'm sorry. But she was--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you did.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't. No, I didn't. This is tape number three of Anna Fischer, November 1, 1994, in Los Angeles, California. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Are we sure?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, we were talking. I didn't. Once we started, I realized that we didn't do it. But it'll be OK. We'll survive. No, it's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You were talking about the fact that you never knew where she-- she got the orange, and how she got it, and when she got.

speaker: interviewee
text: And how she talked of how she got the-- the money for it, you know-- no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after this? Where-- how-- how were you-- where-- what happened to your family at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that at this point-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --two of my brothers, I think they were-- one day, I came home. And my mother said that they were-- that some Germans, you know, were rounding up young boys in the-- in the ghetto. And they took them. But they went-- [SIGHS] --but they-- they came. I don't remember how long they were gone-- maybe a week, maybe less, I think. Anyway, they did come back. And then started to think about-- [SIGHS] --then-- then came, you know-- it was getting later and later in '41-- I think in '42. I don't remember exactly those dates. And the Judenrat, I mean, you know about the Judenrat-- the Jewish councils. They were, you know, announcing that, you know, if you worked for the Germans, that there were green cards, and red cards, and pink cards, and whatever cards you know. And-- and if you are fortunate enough and had some--

speaker: interviewer
text: A skill?

speaker: interviewee
text: --connections-- [LAUGHS] --you'd-- you better get yourself a card. And you will, you know, the work, and, you know, so you will be sure that you are not going to be sent away to a work camp, or what have you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember, my sister, and I, and my cousins, we did get some cards. We were supposed to work. There was this organization, Todt Organization, which was building roads. And-- and what-- the Germans were being-- getting ready for the invasion of the-- of Russia. Of course, we didn't know that. And so, my sister and I, we did get cards to work on the stone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I-- it was so-- I mean, it-- it was so senseless and useless. We were, really, what-- maybe, I don't know. They had a-- oh, I don't know-- a group maybe of 100 women-- young girls. And we would carry rocks from one place to another-- I don't know-- 5 meters, 10 meters. And sometimes we would carry them back. Anyway, this was-- this was our work. And this was supposed-- if and when any-- start-- people started talking about-- not so much deportation, but, you know, sending people. Because this was-- [SIGHS] --I think that was-- there was one. This was Chelmno, I think. That was-- they-- they started sending people to Chelmno. And Chelmno was the first death camp. Maybe you know that. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you know what was going on in Chelmno? Or you just knew--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't-- we did not know. There were all kinds of rumors. And some-- we-- besides, you know, that's another thing-- that we did not know exactly what-- I mean, that everybody-- that any Jew is being persecuted wherever he was. I mean, you had it on your skin. But whether they are being killed in those camps-- until the first-- until-- we heard it first, actually, when-- when the first deportation from-- from Miedzyrzec took place and people were sent to-- to Treblinka. But-- but you heard. And some of the things, you know-- and then-- and then war was going on. And people were-- everything talking about me or with a-- I remember my father had a-- you know, my father was sort of a, you know, very highly-- like, otherwise, I mean, he was a good-- he was a very-- very religious Jew. And he was a good-- I mean, a learned Jew. And he had a friend. And, you know, they called him Rabbi Alta. His first name was Alta Lipschitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: His first name was Alta. And they would-- this man-- I remember him like now-- how-- like now. He would come in every morning, you know, with some rumors. And he would say-- Rabbi Alta-- you don't understand Yiddish, or do you? A little bit. Anyway, whatever he would say, he would say Rabbi Alta, [NON-ENGLISH]. You-- you understand that? But you should say it in English, only because somebody may look at this someday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, Mr. Lipschitz, Mr. Alta, don't worry. We will-- we will-- what? We will outlive them. That's exactly-- actually, sort of the-- the literal translation. [LAUGHTER] What they-- we have them underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father would-- my father was getting to be very despondent, because he was so religious. And he just couldn't see why all is happening, you know. What did we-- did we really sin so much? And-- and my father would also say, well, but who knows if they won't get rid of us before they are gone, you know? [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyway, so that was going on for a-- for a while. Then came-- that keeps on getting-- then came 1942. I think I remember so little.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. You just can tell us what you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I will. But I-- because I remember, you know, at that time, there was-- it was fairly quiet in this area. I mean, you didn't have what to eat, and you were afraid to be caught by the-- by the-- by the German. Because he would beat you up, or he will send you away, or he would put a bullet through your head. We-- we knew all this, and everybody-- well, maybe not everybody-- but we were really starving. And I remember, you know, in-- in there developed-- this is maybe only because of those of us who had so little left. My-- developed a certain-- I don't know-- bread baking industry. This I-- I-- this-- I know. You bought-- I don't even know who produced it and how they were springing up-- sort of a little mill. This wasn't so little. It was-- I don't know. And we, the girls, it's I-- and this was all also illegal. This was all blackmail. This was all smuggling. We would-- I don't even remember who made this contact. Maybe my aunt, because she-- we would go to this peasant who was selling us the grain-- let's say, 50 kilograms or maybe 30 kilograms. I mean, it really-- I remember it was-- they had-- every-- very early in the morning, when we are hoping, you know, that we won't get any German-- to meet any German. And we were bringing back this grain, and the-- actually, the boys were-- were grinding it. You know, we lived in one room. You can imagine how this room looked. We were all covered with this floury dust. It didn't come out such a fine grain. And then we had-- again, I imagine, you know, my brothers or my father-- I don't know how this contact was made. Because I wasn't involved anymore. We were-- we were delivering it to a-- to a baker. And that was how we, at that time, more or less earned some money. So-- and we were not only one. I don't know how many. Of course, people who were from Miedzyrzec know some of them were still, you know, quite well-to-do, I suppose. And-- and I-- and I think that probably till now-- but they-- they destroyed most of the houses that we were in in Miedzyrzec, in '84. Especially the ghetto was all gone. But I am sure that-- that people were talking about it-- that the walls there were just full of furs. And people were hoping that after the war, they will take them out. Because these were those fur merchants.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this I remember. And that's how this sort of-- sort of went, you know, that the beginning of 1942. And then this-- and then my sister-- where was she? No, this was later, after the first deportation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my sister and I, we would-- though people sort of used us as-- as communicators. You know, I would go several times to the Biala Podlaska. I would go. There was [NON-ENGLISH], another city. People would have friends there or would have people assumed they left some. Once, I know I brought-- which-- but it was the night of-- of the deportation from Biala Podlaska. And there was this one family who moved to-- to Miedzyrzec. I think Biala was being-- but this was not totally apparently juden-- made judenrein before. Because one of the boy-- one of the sons of this family in Biala Podlaska, he got engaged to one of my cousins. They never got married either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he disturbing you? After he say-- I mean, they never got married. But his family must have been a pretty-- and he, since he was born then, he probably also, like my cousin, [NON-ENGLISH], he knew all those Poles and probably some of the Germans also. So he had a permit to travel. I don't know if he was part of the Judenrat maybe. I really don't know. So, but this was really interesting, because he came to us and said, would I go to Biala? And he knew. He was notified that the following morning, at 6 o'clock, the deportation from Biala is going to take place. So he's going to go there, too, as a Jew, with his permit. I should go without them.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I don't need any permit. I should go at the same time. Of course, we-- I was travelling together. And of course, see, I had this-- I had this where I'm supposed to-- to go. And he will come there too. And here is this one in cahoots. One of them-- I don't know if it was a German or a Pole. I don't remember anymore. He said we should wait in this apartment until the tip will come. Bring me a suitcase and-- about 4 o'clock in the morning before, you know, the deportation starts. Then we should-- and he will with by himself also. But we will go to the train and come back to meet. And we did. And this night-- I-- oh, it was one of those nights we'll never forget. We were-- we both, at that time-- you know this was-- this must have been when the deportation from Warsaw started-- around probably, July 1942. And we were sitting all night long and philosophizing. Is-- he was quite intelligent. I said, boy. Now I've-- I don't know if I was so intelligent. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But anyway, I was, you know, I was considered quite well educated. And we were talking whether-- you know, I remember we talked about Spinoza. We talked about whether the struggle is all worth it. What is life really about?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and why is it-- you know, we-- really, when I think of it now that we had-- we talked all night long. We were just sitting there and talking. And of course, now you know, our hearts are probably going like sitting about-- about maybe. About 3 o'clock in the morning, maybe, we heard-- you know, they started shooting. And then this-- some man that-- I think he was a Pole-- came in and brought this suitcase, and left, and told us, in 10 minutes or in 15 minutes we would walk out-- I mean, not together, of course-- and go to the railway station and-- and come to-- if all goes well, pick up. Then I found out-- and, you know, and that was bad-- that I was-- we were talking, and talking, and talking. And then when we came. We took the train and everything and went. And there I found out that this suitcase was full of money-- I don't know, maybe dollars. I don't know. You know, dollars was a commodity, which was-- and, you know, and this fellow, he was so happy that everything went pass.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we arrived, it was early in the morning. So he insisted that he wants to treat me to breakfast. And before I even-- this, I remember. I felt so badly. Before I even went home to the-- but I'm-- you know, later on when I was considering, and now when I think of it, too-- though, for my-- for my mother-- my father was not alive anymore. For-- no, he was still alive that time until the 24th of August.

speaker: interviewee
text: But for my parents, you know, this waiting was much more. Because I was in the situation. So whatever happens I-- you know, and here, when I came home, maybe by then it was 10 o'clock, maybe-- in the morning. I have never seen my parents so angry-- and rightly so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the-- who do you think you are? What, did you lose your head? Don't you have any sense anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: You think that because you looked like a shiksa, you can do-- I mean, they were really so angry. And I felt-- I-- even till now, when I think of this moment-- stupid thing. So this was my-- [LAUGHS] --my adventures of the-- of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When Biala became judenrein-- and nothing happened. And then, of course, things were getting-- then you heard, you know, people had-- but you know, in a funny way-- Warsaw was being liquidated. I mean, it was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this you knew? This you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this we knew. But, you know, in a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in a funny way, people don't really-- and I remember, I kept on saying, it probably is so, because you see Jewish life is not worth a penny. Even in Miedzyrzec, there was this one who was a head of the German police, I think. He was called Schlager. This wasn't his name. But he was called Schlager, because he was beating right and left everybody. And that was-- and for him to take out a gun and kill a person, a Jewish person, didn't mean anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, look. If you-- you sometimes you saw there were those-- they have Jewish police. They were mostly young. Some of them maybe thought that they are going to help, you know, their families, at least, if they become policeman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some, I'm sure-- look. There are people among the Jews, like among everybody else. There were thieves before the war. There were murderers. There were the meanest-- all kinds of characters. Some of them like this became also policemen. They suddenly got power into their hands, you know. They are directing others how to live and what to do. But the Gestapo, after about two or three months-- would-- and-- and some of them even knew by then-- would take this whole contingent of the Jewish police, take them somewheres, and kill them, and then pick up a contingent of new ones. And I-- when I think of it, I don't remember what I was doing there. And I didn't even know it, you know, that those things are happening. But I remember I passed by. And there were-- there was sort of a hill. And there were the Jewish police guarded on this hill. And I didn't know for what. But then when I-- a day later or so-- those rumors, again. You know, there was no-- there was no-- there was no press. There is no-- everything was-- there was no radio. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: There was-- and sometimes you wondered, I mean, how much of all those things that you hear are really so? And then people kept on saying all the time, the world cannot-- cannot allow Hitler to do those things. How can they allow those things like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the same thing with this police. And later, you found out that this contingent was eliminated. And they picked some new ones. And besides, there was this-- and this I remember also, because I was. But this was already after the-- [SIGHS] --the second or the third deportation. The first deportation from Miedzyrzec took place on the-- the-- I mean, the 25th of-- of August, 1942. And the evening-- this was with my father and my two brothers who lost their life. And about 4 or 5 o'clock in the afternoon-- or maybe a little bit later-- started this rumor. There was always some Nazi who was always in cahoots with some Jews from the-- from the Judenrat, I imagine. Started those rumors that somebody heard that the-- and then the deportation was in full swing already. If you read ordinary-- I mean, you-- you know that, I suppose. Or maybe your don't pay attention. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It started, though, the rumors that the deportation will take place from Miedzyrzec. And people really, you know, became so panicky. People started running back and forth. And-- and what shall we do? Shall we try to hide? Shall we try to run away? You know, there are those meadows-- fields, around Miedzyrzec.

speaker: interviewee
text: And anyway, before you knew what is happening, those-- you know, we called them all Gestapos. They were not even Gestapos. They were those-- a special reserve unit that were brought for this particular-- they didn't even know what they are coming there for. They were spread all over the-- the place and started shooting whoever they saw. And you know, we lived in this room, which was like a store front. You know, it had-- what was armoire one room, and it has a door into the-- into the sidewalk, into the street in that-- a door and the-- and suddenly-- I remember like now. My-- the men, you know, were still-- and-- and you always thought that the men are-- you didn't know that the are you know, you are going to death camps. And you always thought that the men are-- are threatened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they started talking, you know, before. I mean, what shall we do? Maybe shall we run away? Shall we do? Shall we do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and also, my-- my aunt wasn't alive anymore; my cousins. And before you knew, somebody just tore this door open, pulled out my father and my two brothers, and just put bullets through their heads. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So one survives everything--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] --if one lives. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then with-- with some help with some neighbor, we went to an attic. And we were lying there for three days while the deportation took place. And then we came down. And-- and our little room was-- we just-- the Poles just helped themselves to whatever there was, you know, and-- skirt, a blouse, a pot. There was nothing left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was-- this I remember exactly. And then my cousin, he was-- he was, you know-- he was-- he was really a fighter-- poor fellow-- that he did not survive. So he said, well, we are not going to-- and my uncle was also killed that night. My-- you know, the wife of my aunt. So he decided, no. We are going. We are going to dig ourselves a hiding place somewheres in the fields. Nobody will know where it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there where we will wait until the end of the war. It's easier said than done. But we did. It was really a very, like, a deep hole. And of course, as you know, those things were always-- everything was planned, you know. That my sister and I, we will be going out at night and go into the villages. And they want, you know, we will give them. We will pay for them. And they will, you know, give us some food. And we can stay there, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then probably-- and this was, what? This was August, and came-- no, September was quiet, I think. But that was those-- those plans were done. And we are going to go there and wait until-- I don't know what-- until doomsday? And we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were-- you know, then some people found out about it. And there were more and more people being, you know-- wanted to. And-- and then through the month of September, nothing happened. There was no deportation until October 3rd. Deportation took place. And most of the people in the town were taken away. But I don't remember. But that we were there all this time, and my sister and I, we would walk out. Or sometimes we would, you know, travel to sell-- I don't know what-- a bracelet, a ring, maybe, if there was something left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and how was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, and then, yeah, now I remember. While the deportation took place on the 3rd of October, I wasn't there. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because after the first deportation, a friend of mine, [NON-ENGLISH], she-- she, again, started, you know, we should-- we should start work-- we should work. Now they changed the color of the Judenrat. And, you know, the Germans need the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we joined one of those work groups. And we were driving out every morning. This was also on-- with trucks-- I don't know, about 12, 15 kilometers. I don't remember-- and digging something again. You know, we were working on the field.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, when we were at work, and my sister was away-- I don't know where she was-- you know, again, traveling to smuggle something. And then we heard rumors about 11 o'clock in the morning. This was also an interesting incident. That there's going to be a deportation from Miedzyrzec. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then maybe-- so we know, it really started a big again, panic. Everybody said, what shall we do? What? They probably won't take us, because we are working, blah, blah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And before you knew, there came, maybe two trucks, with German gendarmes. This was the people from this book. And I probably would have just stood there and waited for them to take me. I'm-- I'm almost sure. But my friend, [NON-ENGLISH], she grabbed me by my arm. And we started running-- the two of us. And not far from there, there was a farmhouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was really so intelligent-- [LAUGHS] --enterprising. And so we ran into the barn of this farmhouse. We went up quickly to the attic. We buried ourselves in the hay and were lying there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we heard shooting. And we heard screams. And we heard-- and there we were lying. And then it got dark, and it quieted down. And there we were, still safe. And we had no idea what to do. And then, it must have been late-- it was dark already. It must have been pretty late. We hear somebody comes to the barn and says-- see, this was another-- we never-- we never took his name. I have no idea who it was. And he says, if anybody is here, I am leaving some milk and bread. And I will come back tomorrow morning. And if this bread and milk are gone, I assume that someone is there, and I will be coming back every evening with milk and bread. And we were lying there. And the deportation was taking place. And then the ghetto was-- you know, people, they want-- the people who were, again, they were hiding, like my mother did with my little sister, with some neighbors, in-- somewhere in the basement of something or other. But the ghetto was closed for three weeks. There was not a soul to be-- to be seen. They kept it-- maybe they thought, you know, that everybody is gone already. I don't know-- or waited to make sure that [NON-ENGLISH] became judenrein. And we were lying there. My feet burn-- froze. My-- that's another thing. My-- my toes froze, as [NON-ENGLISH]. And she kept on saying, don't worry. Don't worry. We won't be sitting-- lying here all the time. You know, she was-- and we were going down in about midnight. And there was this milk and bread. And that's what we lived on. And maybe it must have been already November.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this went on for several weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: This went on for at least three weeks. Nobody-- nothing was happening. It was absolutely quiet. We didn't know what's happening in town. We didn't know whether the-- that the ghetto is still. We knew, also, that nobody-- there are no Jews on the road-- you know, this contingent that we were a part of. And one day we hear the truck. And we hear Jewish voices. And it-- it was really a cold day. It was freezing already. And we look through the top there on the-- and we see there are Jews working on the road again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm going to stop you here so we can change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. See, I will never--
